text: The prepared left colon and sigmoid colon are visible in the surgical field during a low anterior resection for rectal cancer.
The mesocolon is visibly divided at the level of the rectosigmoid junction, showing clear separation of the tissue layers in this area.
The surgeon identifies a suitable point for colonic transection at the descending colon, preparing to divide the colon proximally during the procedure.
The surgeon administers five mLs of indocyanine green to assess perfusion at the proximal colonic transection point. The fluorescence under white light laparoscopy indicates satisfactory perfusion.
The surgeon observes the uptake of indocyanine green dye in the small bowel using near-infrared laparoscopic fluorescence, highlighting the perfusion in this area.
The surgeon observes an absence of fluorescence in the descending colon, indicating a lack of perfusion in this area during the low anterior resection for rectal cancer.
The surgical team identifies a point approximately ten centimeters more proximal than the previously selected site for colonic transection.

Under white light laparoscopy, the surgeon observes a lack of perfusion in the segment of the colon initially selected for transection, which was intended to be the proximal side of the colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon re-prepares the colonic mesocolon at a more proximal point, positioning the stapler to secure the proximal transection within a viable zone, ensuring adequate perfusion.
The colonic segment now shows excellent perfusion, indicating adequate blood flow in the area.
The surgeon observes satisfactory perfusion within the rectal remnant and performs the distal transection at the recto sigmoid junction.
The surgeon removes the specimen and places an anvil within the proximal colonic segment outside the body, preparing for the colorectal anastomosis.
The circular stapler is placed transanally and advanced through the rectal stump, preparing for the colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon mates the anvil with the stapler head to perform the colorectal anastomosis, aligning the two components for secure connection.
Indocyanine green is administered to assess the perfusion of the in situ colorectal anastomosis, with fluorescence indicating adequate blood flow.
Fluorescence imaging shows satisfactory perfusion in both the colonic and rectal segments, indicating adequate blood flow to these areas.
The surgeon confirms the placement and perfusion of the colorectal anastomosis using a near-infrared view, highlighting the fluorescence of the indocyanine green.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis to visually confirm the mechanical integrity of the connection.
